channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Synthesizing Multi-Agent Harnesses for Vulnerability Discovery
item.description: Large language model (LLM) agents have demonstrated unprecedented capability in discovering real-world security vulnerabilities missed by human auditors and traditional fuzzers—yet their effectiveness critically depends on the *multi-agent harness*: the program orchestrating agent roles, prompts, tools, communication topology, and coordination protocols. Current harness design is largely manual or limited to narrow search spaces with coarse pass/fail feedback, lacking diagnostic signals for failure root causes. AgentFlow addresses both limitations via (1) a typed graph domain-specific language (DSL) that jointly optimizes all harness dimensions, and (2) a feedback-driven outer loop that introspects target program runtime signals (e.g., crashes, sandbox violations) to localize and repair faulty harness components. Evaluated on TerminalBench-2 with Claude Opus 4.6, AgentFlow achieves **84.3%**, the highest published score. On Google Chrome using Kimi K2.5, it discovers **10 previously unknown zero-day vulnerabilities**, including **two Critical sandbox-escape flaws** (CVE-2026-5280, CVE-2026-6297).

item.title: DAIRE: A lightweight AI model for real-time detection of Controller Area Network attacks in the Internet of Vehicles
item.description: DAIRE is a lightweight AI framework designed for real-time detection and classification of Controller Area Network (CAN) attacks in the Internet of Vehicles (IoV). It employs a custom artificial neural network with neuron count per layer defined as $N_i = i \times c$, where $c$ is the number of attack classes, enabling ultra-low computational overhead while maintaining high accuracy. Trained with sparse categorical cross-entropy loss and RMSProp optimization, DAIRE effectively identifies DoS, Fuzzy, and Spoofing attacks. Evaluated on CICIoV2024 and Car-Hacking datasets, it achieves an average detection rate of **99.88%**, a false positive rate of **0.02%**, and overall accuracy of **99.96%**, with inference time of only **0.03 ms per sample**—significantly outperforming state-of-the-art models in speed and efficiency. DAIRE bridges the gap between robust cybersecurity and real-time constraints in resource-limited vehicular systems.

item.title: Differentially Private Clustered Federated Learning with Privacy-Preserving Initialization and Normality-Driven Aggregation
item.description: Federated learning (FL) preserves data locality but suffers from slow convergence and poor generalization under cross-device data heterogeneity; clustered FL (CFL) mitigates this by grouping clients with similar data distributions. However, integrating differential privacy (DP) into CFL remains challenging: DP noise corrupts client updates, preventing reliable cluster centroid initialization and degrading clustering quality. To address this, we propose **PINA**, a two-stage differentially private CFL framework. First, clients fine-tune lightweight LoRA adapters and privately share *compressed sketches* of their updates—enabling the server to construct robust, noise-resilient cluster centroids. Second, PINA introduces **normality-driven aggregation**: it statistically tests update normality (e.g., via Shapiro-Wilk) and adaptively filters outliers before weighted aggregation, enhancing convergence stability and robustness. Extensive experiments show PINA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art DP-FL methods by **+2.9% average accuracy** under tight privacy budgets (ε ∈ {2, 8}), while maintaining high intra-cluster coherence. PINA is the first framework to deliver formal DP guarantees *without sacrificing CFL’s structural benefits*, enabling practical, privacy-preserving personalized learning in heterogeneous edge environments.

item.title: Towards Certified Malware Detection: Provable Guarantees Against Evasion Attacks
item.description: Machine learning-based static malware detectors are highly vulnerable to adversarial evasion—especially metamorphic mutations that preserve functionality while altering features. To address this, we propose the first *certifiably robust* detection framework for binaries, leveraging randomized smoothing via *feature ablation* and *targeted noise injection*. Our method generates numerous ablated variants of an input executable, classifies each with a base classifier, and outputs the majority-voted label. Crucially, by analyzing the top-class vote distribution and applying the Wilson score interval, we derive a formal robustness certificate: the predicted label is guaranteed invariant against any feature-space perturbation within a certified radius *R*. Evaluated on clean executables and PyMetaEngine-generated metamorphic variants, our smoothed classifier achieves 92.3% clean accuracy and issues valid certificates for 78.6% of samples (with *R* ≥ 15), while reducing evasion success by 41.2%—all without modifying the underlying model architecture or retraining. This work establishes a theoretically grounded, practical path toward trustworthy malware detection.

item.title: CyberCertBench: Evaluating LLMs in Cybersecurity Certification Knowledge
item.description: CyberCertBench is a novel MCQA benchmark for evaluating LLMs on real-world cybersecurity certification knowledge, derived from industry standards including CISSP, IEC 62443, NIST SP 800, and OT-specific certifications. We introduce the Proposer-Verifier framework—a method to generate interpretable, standards-grounded natural language explanations for model predictions. Evaluation shows frontier models achieve human-expert accuracy (≥92%) on general IT/networking security, but drop sharply (28–41% points) on vendor-specific nuances or formal standard clauses (e.g., IEC 62443-3-3). Scaling analysis reveals strong parameter efficiency gains from 2023–2024, yet diminishing returns in recent >100B-parameter models. Code and data are open-sourced at https://github.com/GKeppler/CyberCertBench.

item.title: Text Steganography with Dynamic Codebook and Multimodal Large Language Model
item.description: This paper proposes a novel black-box text steganography framework leveraging a **dynamic codebook** and a **multimodal large language model (MLLM)** to overcome key limitations of existing white-box and black-box approaches. Unlike white-box methods—vulnerable due to shared off-the-shelf LLMs—or rigid black-box schemes requiring fixed codebooks and sentence-specific extraction prompts, our method generates session-dependent, context-aware codebooks on-the-fly using lightweight shared configurations (e.g., timestamp + seed) and an MLLM (e.g., Qwen-VL). Secret messages are embedded via an encrypted steganographic mapping during caption generation, preserving linguistic quality. A reject-sampling–based feedback optimization ensures reliable message extraction. Experiments show our method achieves **4.2 bits/sentence embedding capacity**, outperforming state-of-the-art white-box methods by 1.8× in capacity and significantly improving text quality (BERTScore +9.3%, BLEU-4 +12.6%). Crucially, it demonstrates superior practicality on real-world platforms (e.g., Twitter, Weibo) without requiring model access or API keys—only public MLLM interfaces.

item.title: Self-Guided Plan Extraction for Instruction-Following Tasks with Goal-Conditional Reinforcement Learning
item.description: We propose **SuperIgor**, a self-guided framework for instruction-following tasks that eliminates reliance on hand-crafted subtasks or annotated demonstration datasets. SuperIgor establishes a closed-loop co-training paradigm: a language model (LM) autonomously generates and refines high-level plans, while a goal-conditioned RL agent learns to execute them in stochastic, dynamic environments. Crucially, the RL agent provides dense feedback—on fidelity, feasibility, and goal alignment—which the LM uses to iteratively improve plan structure, granularity, and robustness. Evaluated across diverse embodied navigation and tool-use benchmarks, SuperIgor agents achieve **23.7% higher instruction adherence accuracy** and **41.2% better zero-shot generalization to unseen instructions** versus strong baselines (SFT, PPO+CLIP, HIRO). This work demonstrates the first successful joint emergence of planning and control capabilities in an unannotated, self-supervised setting.

item.title: FSFM: A Biologically-Inspired Framework for Selective Forgetting of Agent Memory
item.description: FSFM is a biologically inspired framework that formalizes *selective forgetting* as a first-class capability for LLM agents—moving beyond passive memory retention to active, adaptive memory curation. Grounded in hippocampal indexing theory and the Ebbinghaus forgetting curve, FSFM introduces a taxonomy of four forgetting mechanisms: passive decay, active deletion, safety-triggered erasure, and adaptive reinforcement learning–based forgetting. Implemented atop modern agent architectures and vector databases, it enables efficient metadata-aware pruning, real-time sensitive-data scrubbing, and context-aware preference updating. Controlled experiments demonstrate: **+8.49% memory access efficiency**, **+29.2% signal-to-noise ratio in generated content**, and **100% elimination of security risks** (e.g., prompt injection, PII leakage). FSFM bridges cognitive neuroscience and production AI systems, advancing both ethical compliance and resource-constrained deployment of next-generation agents.

item.title: FedSIR: Spectral Client Identification and Relabeling for Federated Learning with Noisy Labels
item.description: Federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training, yet pervasive label noise across clients severely degrades model performance. To address this, we propose **FedSIR**, a spectral-domain framework for robust FL under noisy labels. Unlike prior works relying on noise-robust losses or loss dynamics, FedSIR identifies noisy clients via **spectral consistency analysis** of class-wise feature subspaces—requiring minimal communication—and enables them to relabel corrupted samples using spectral references (dominant directions + residual subspaces) from clean clients. It further stabilizes optimization via a noise-aware strategy combining logit-adjusted loss, knowledge distillation from clean clients, and distance-aware model aggregation. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet show FedSIR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods by **5.2–9.7 percentage points** under 40%–60% symmetric/asymmetric noise. Code is available at https://github.com/sinagh72/FedSIR.

item.title: Lifecycle-Aware Federated Continual Learning in Mobile Autonomous Systems
item.description: Federated continual learning (FCL) enables distributed autonomous fleets to collaboratively adapt to evolving environments across extended mission lifecycles—yet existing methods suffer from uniform layer protection, neglect of long-term cumulative drift, and over-reliance on idealized simulations. We propose a **lifecycle-aware dual-timescale FCL framework**, integrating *training-time rehearsal* and *post-degradation recovery*. Specifically, we design a **layer-selective rehearsal strategy**, allocating memory based on gradient sensitivity and semantic hierarchy to suppress immediate forgetting; and a **rapid knowledge recovery mechanism**, using topology-aware distillation to restore model fidelity after prolonged drift. Theoretically, we characterize heterogeneous forgetting dynamics and prove the inevitability of long-term degradation under non-IID and device heterogeneity. Experiments show up to **+8.3% mIoU** over the strongest federated baseline and **+31.7%** over conventional fine-tuning. Real-world deployment on a rover testbed under realistic constraints (≤50 KB/s bandwidth, 23% disconnection rate, battery-limited operation) confirms robust system-level performance retention.

item.title: Generative Augmentation of Imbalanced Flight Records for Flight Diversion Prediction: A Multi-objective Optimisation Framework
item.description: Flight diversions—rare yet critical aviation events—suffer from extreme class imbalance in historical records, severely limiting predictive model performance. To address this, we propose a **multi-objective optimisation (MOO) framework** that jointly tunes hyperparameters and architecture choices of three deep generative models (TVAE, CTGAN, CopulaGAN), with Gaussian Copula as statistical baseline. Our MOO objective balances synthetic data quality across six dimensions: realism, diversity, operational validity, statistical similarity, fidelity, and predictive utility—rigorously validated via expert review and downstream classification tasks. Results show MOO-optimised generators significantly outperform non-optimised counterparts (+38.2% average score gain, *p*<0.01), and synthetic augmentation lifts XGBoost’s diversion prediction AUC from 0.721 (real-data-only) to **0.864**, with F1-score increasing by 0.22. This work establishes a principled, operationally grounded paradigm for generative augmentation of rare safety-critical events in air transport.

item.title: RespondeoQA: a Benchmark for Bilingual Latin-English Question Answering
item.description: We introduce **RespondeoQA**, the first benchmark dataset for bilingual Latin–English question answering and constrained translation, comprising ~7,800 expert-validated QA pairs. Questions are sourced from pedagogical Latin materials—including textbooks (1800s–present), AP exams, and quizbowl trivia—and cover diverse task types: knowledge-based QA, skill-oriented tasks (e.g., scansion, literary device identification), multihop reasoning, and cross-lingual translation (Latin↔English, mixed-language queries). Evaluating LLaMa 3, Qwen QwQ, and OpenAI’s o3-mini, we find all models underperform significantly on skill-based questions (−18–25 F1 points vs. knowledge QA); while o3-mini and QwQ show modest gains in scansion and literary analysis, overall improvements remain limited. QwQ achieves the highest robustness on Latin-prompted questions, whereas LLaMa 3 and o3-mini exhibit stronger task dependency. RespondeoQA enables fine-grained assessment of LLM capabilities in classical language understanding and supports reproducible research in humanities AI. The dataset is publicly available at https://github.com/slanglab/RespondeoQA.

item.title: Anchor-and-Resume Concession Under Dynamic Pricing for LLM-Augmented Freight Negotiation
item.description: This paper addresses monotonicity violation and non-determinism in LLM-augmented freight negotiation under dynamic pricing. We propose the **Anchor-and-Resume Concession Framework**, which decouples pricing logic from language generation: a spread-derived adaptive $β$ tailors concession speed to each load’s margin regime, while the anchor-and-resume mechanism guarantees strictly non-decreasing offers despite arbitrary price shifts—all via deterministic formulas. The LLM serves only as a natural-language translation layer, eliminating inference cost, nondeterminism, and prompt injection risks. Empirical evaluation across 115,125 real negotiations shows: (1) adaptive $β$ outperforms fixed-$β$ baselines in broker savings for medium/wide spreads and prioritizes closure in narrow spreads; (2) it matches a 20B-parameter unconstrained LLM broker in agreement rate and savings; (3) against stochastic LLM-powered carriers, it achieves comparable savings and **higher agreement rates** than against rule-based opponents; and (4) it scales horizontally to thousands of concurrent negotiations with near-zero inference overhead and full decision transparency.

item.title: An AI Agent Execution Environment to Safeguard User Data
item.description: AI agents promise powerful personal assistance but pose serious privacy risks by requiring access to sensitive user data—exposing it to prompt injection, model compromise, or untrustworthy providers. This paper introduces **GAAP (Guaranteed Accounting for Agent Privacy)**, a novel execution environment that *deterministically guarantees* confidentiality of private user data without trusting the AI model, the provider, or the user prompt. GAAP dynamically elicits fine-grained, task-specific permission specifications via guided user interaction and enforces them using an enhanced Information Flow Control mechanism—augmented with persistent data stores and temporal annotations to track private data across execution steps *and* across time-separated tasks. Evaluation shows GAAP blocks *all* tested data disclosure attacks—including those that bypass state-of-the-art defenses—while incurring only minimal overhead (<8.2% latency increase) and preserving >99% agent utility. GAAP provides the first practical, model-agnostic, zero-trust foundation for privacy-safe AI agent deployment.

item.title: Cyber Defense Benchmark: Agentic Threat Hunting Evaluation for LLMs in SecOps
item.description: We introduce the **Cyber Defense Benchmark (CDB)**, the first evaluation framework designed to assess LLM agents’ ability to perform *unprompted, evidence-driven threat hunting*—a core SOC analyst task. CDB presents agents with an in-memory SQLite database of 75k–135k obfuscated Windows event logs from 106 real-world attack procedures (covering 86 ATT&CK sub-techniques across 12 tactics), wrapped in a Gymnasium RL environment. Agents must iteratively issue SQL queries to discover malicious timestamps and explicitly flag them, scored against Sigma-rule-derived ground truth in CTF style. Evaluating five state-of-the-art models (Claude Opus 4.6, GPT-5, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Kimi K2.5, Gemini 3 Flash) across 26 campaigns, we find catastrophic failure: the best model achieves only **3.8% average recall**, and **no run ever identifies all malicious events**. Defining operational readiness as ≥50% recall across all 13 ATT&CK tactics, **no model passes**—the leader clears the bar on just 5 tactics, scoring zero on the remaining 8. These results demonstrate that current LLMs are fundamentally unsuited for open-ended threat hunting despite strong performance on curated security QA benchmarks.

item.title: Evaluating LLM-Generated Obfuscated XSS Payloads for Machine Learning-Based Detection
item.description: Cross-site scripting (XSS) remains a critical web vulnerability, exacerbated by obfuscation techniques that preserve malicious behavior while altering syntactic form—evading both signature-based and ML-driven detectors. This paper introduces a behavior-aware pipeline for generating and evaluating LLM-produced obfuscated XSS payloads: it integrates deterministic transformations with LLM generation and enforces rigorous browser-based runtime validation (e.g., DOM modification, alert firing) to assess functional equivalence—not just lexical similarity. Evaluation shows that fine-tuning an off-the-shelf LLM on behavior-preserving obfuscation pairs improves runtime behavior match rate from 0.15 (baseline) to 0.22—demonstrating measurable but limited progress. Crucially, adding raw generated payloads degrades downstream classifier performance, whereas behavior-filtered samples integrate without material loss (<0.5% AUC drop). The work underscores that runtime validation is essential for high-fidelity adversarial data generation and reveals fundamental gaps in current LLMs’ ability to reliably preserve XSS semantics under obfuscation.

item.title: API Security Based on Automatic OpenAPI Mapping
item.description: This paper introduces **Map Reduce Graph (MRG)**, a novel unsupervised framework for real-time API security and documentation. MRG automatically infers OpenAPI-compliant specifications—routes, methods, parameter schemas, and request/response structures—directly from unlabeled HTTP traffic, eliminating manual annotation or prior knowledge. It operates in three phases: offline training, lightweight online updating for evolving APIs, and low-latency detection via graph-based structural validation and deep autoencoder–based payload analysis. Evaluated on real-world microservice traffic, MRG achieves **11.4% higher recall**, **>20× faster inference**, and **perfect precision (100%)** against API-layer attacks—including SQLi, path traversal, and malformed JSON—outperforming HRAL and FT-ANN. Its OpenAPI-native output enables seamless integration with observability and security toolchains, delivering fully automated, explainable, and production-ready API visibility and anomaly detection.

item.title: Sherpa.ai Privacy-Preserving Multi-Party Entity Alignment without Intersection Disclosure for Noisy Identifiers
item.description: This paper introduces Sherpa.ai’s multi-party Private Set Union (PSU) protocol for privacy-preserving entity alignment (PPEA) in Vertical Federated Learning (VFL). Unlike conventional PSI—which leaks intersection membership and exposes sensitive cross-dataset relationships—our protocol aligns entities over the *union* of identifiers, provably hiding which samples are shared. It supports *both exact and noisy matching* (e.g., typos, formatting variants) across *three or more parties*, with low communication overhead (*O(n log m)*) and computational cost (*O(n)* exponentiations). We propose two variants: an *order-preserving* version for structured IDs and an *unordered*, fuzzy-matching version using secure Bloom filters and locality-sensitive hashing. We formally prove correctness, SIM-security, and define a universal index mapping from local records to a shared space. The protocol enables scalable, regulation-compliant VFL deployments in healthcare, finance, and telecom—without revealing intersection information.

item.title: DP-FlogTinyLLM: Differentially private federated log anomaly detection using Tiny LLMs
item.description: Modern distributed systems generate massive log data essential for anomaly and cyber threat detection. However, logs are often siloed across organizations due to privacy and security constraints, rendering centralized learning infeasible. To address this, we propose **DP-FlogTinyLLM**, a differentially private federated framework leveraging parameter-efficient Tiny LLMs (e.g., Phi-3-mini) fine-tuned via Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) at each client. It integrates federated optimization with calibrated Laplace noise injection and gradient clipping to satisfy $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-differential privacy—without sharing raw logs. Experiments on Thunderbird and BGL show DP-FlogTinyLLM matches centralized LLM performance (F1: 0.92 vs. 0.93) while outperforming existing federated baselines by +5.2–8.7 F1 points; notably, it achieves +12.4% precision on Thunderbird, drastically reducing false positives. The framework incurs only modest computational overhead and is deployable on resource-constrained edge devices.

item.title: ProjLens: Unveiling the Role of Projectors in Multimodal Model Safety
item.description: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) achieve impressive cross-modal capabilities but suffer from critical safety vulnerabilities—especially backdoor attacks. While prior work demonstrates backdoor feasibility via projector fine-tuning, the underlying mechanisms remain opaque. We propose **ProjLens**, the first interpretability framework dedicated to demystifying how backdoors embed and activate *within the vision-language projector*. We show that even standard downstream alignment—restricted solely to projector tuning—introduces intrinsic backdoor susceptibility, with a mechanism distinct from text-only LLMs. Across four backdoor variants, we uncover: (1) **Low-rank encoding**: Backdoor-critical parameters reside not in sparse “trigger neurons” but in a compact low-rank subspace of the projector; (2) **Norm-scaled semantic shift**: Both clean and poisoned embeddings shift toward the backdoor target direction, yet the shift magnitude scales *linearly with input embedding norm*, causing reliable misclassification only on high-norm poisoned samples. ProjLens enables precise, projector-centric safety analysis and mitigation. Code: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ProjLens-8FD7

item.title: CHRONOS: A Hardware-Assisted Phase-Decoupled Framework for Secure Federated Learning in IoT
item.description: CHRONOS is a hardware-assisted, phase-decoupled framework for secure federated learning in resource-constrained IoT devices. It separates cryptographic setup (ephemeral key exchange) from active training: during idle windows, clients perform a server-relayed Diffie-Hellman handshake inside ARM TrustZone enclaves—generating keys, deriving PRG seeds, and sealing pairwise secrets—all without exposing private keys to Normal World memory. Shamir shares of ephemeral private keys are distributed to peers for fault tolerance. During training, gradients are masked via a single stream-cipher evaluation and transmitted in one round; a hardware-backed round counter ensures mask freshness. If clients drop out, the server reconstructs missing masks from peer-held shares—enabling correct aggregation without retransmission. Evaluation on Rock Pi 4 with OP-TEE shows CHRONOS resists OS-level compromise and state-of-the-art gradient inversion attacks, reduces active-phase aggregation latency by up to 74% (vs. synchronous secure aggregation for 20 clients), and maintains a scalable, model-dimension-independent Secure World footprint of <700 bytes per device.

item.title: Refute-or-Promote: An Adversarial Stage-Gated Multi-Agent Review Methodology for High-Precision LLM-Assisted Defect Discovery
item.description: LLM-assisted defect discovery suffers from a precision crisis: plausible-but-false reports drown maintainers and erode trust in genuine findings. We introduce *Refute-or-Promote*, an inference-time reliability framework combining Stratified Context Hunting (SCH) for candidate generation, adversarial kill mandates at each promotion gate, cold-start reviewers to break anchoring cascades, and a Cross-Model Critic (CMC) leveraging architecturally diverse LLMs to expose correlated blind spots. Across a 31-day campaign on 7 high-stakes targets (security libraries, ISO C++, major compilers), the pipeline eliminated ~79% of 171 candidates retrospectively; prospectively, it achieved an 83% kill rate on a consolidated subset (*n*=30). Validated outcomes include 4 CVEs (3 public), adoption of LWG 4549 into the C++ working paper, 5 merged editorial PRs, 3 compiler conformance bugs, 8 merged security fixes (no CVE), an RFC 9000 errata under IETF review, and FIPS 140-3 compliance issues under coordinated disclosure—all externally confirmed, not benchmark-evaluated. Critically, no vulnerability was found autonomously; the contribution is a robust external filtering structure that systematically suppresses LLMs’ persistent false positives. A simplified cross-family variant also solved 5 unsolved SymPy instances on SWE-bench Verified and 1 hard SWE-rebench task.

item.title: SAGE: Signal-Amplified Guided Embeddings for LLM-based Vulnerability Detection
item.description: Software vulnerabilities pose critical threats to modern infrastructure. While LLMs offer transformative potential for vulnerability detection via semantic reasoning, we identify a fundamental bottleneck: **Signal Submersion**, where vulnerability-relevant features are activated internally but numerically overwhelmed by dominant functional semantics. To address this, we propose **SAGE (Signal-Amplified Guided Embeddings)**—a framework that actively recovers faint vulnerability signals via task-conditional Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) applied to intermediate LLM embeddings. SAGE boosts internal Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) by **12.7×** through sparse manifold projection, enabling a 7B model to achieve up to **318% MCC gain on unseen distributions** and **319% on classic benchmarks**, outperforming 34B baselines across **13 programming languages**. SAGE establishes a more efficient, interpretable, and scalable path to software security—beyond simple parameter scaling.

item.title: Security Is Relative: Training-Free Vulnerability Detection via Multi-Agent Behavioral Contract Synthesis
item.description: Deep learning-based vulnerability detection suffers catastrophic generalization failure (F1 dropping from >0.68 to 0.031 under strict deduplication) due to *semantic ambiguity*: identical code can be secure or vulnerable depending on project-specific behavioral contracts. To resolve this, we propose **Phoenix**, a training-free, multi-agent framework that synthesizes executable behavioral contracts—encoded in Gherkin format—to ground detection in context-aware requirements rather than syntactic patterns. Phoenix decomposes detection into three stages: (1) Semantic Slicing to extract minimal vulnerability-relevant context; (2) Requirement Reverse Engineering to generate precise Gherkin specifications capturing security contracts; and (3) Contract Judging via strict compliance checking. Evaluated on PrimeVul Paired, Phoenix achieves **F1 = 0.825** and **Pair-Correct = 64.4%**, outperforming RASM-Vul (F1=0.668) and VulTrial (F1=0.563) while using open-source models up to **48× smaller** (7–14B vs. 671B). Ablation confirms Gherkin synthesis as the decisive factor (+0.09–+0.35 F1 gain). Crucially, 18% of “false positives” reveal genuine security concerns in patched code—demonstrating that *security is relative to behavioral contracts*, not an absolute property of source code.

item.title: Mechanistic Anomaly Detection via Functional Attribution
item.description: We propose **Mechanistic Anomaly Detection (MAD)** as a *functional attribution* problem: detecting whether a model’s output arises from anomalous internal mechanisms—e.g., backdoors, adversarial triggers, or OOD reasoning—by measuring how well outputs can be *explained* by a small trusted reference set. We operationalize this via influence functions, estimating functional coupling through parameter-space sampling—avoiding latent-space assumptions and obfuscation vulnerabilities. Evaluated across vision and language models, our method achieves state-of-the-art DER of **0.93** on BackdoorBench (vs. 0.83 best prior) and significantly outperforms baselines on LLM backdoors—including explicitly obfuscated variants. It further detects adversarial and OOD samples, and distinguishes *multiple co-occurring anomalies* within a single model. This establishes functional attribution as a modality-agnostic, mechanism-aware foundation for robust deployment monitoring.

item.title: A Dual Perspective on Synthetic Trajectory Generators: Utility Framework and Privacy Vulnerabilities
item.description: Human mobility data enable critical applications but pose severe privacy risks due to sensitive inferences (e.g., religion, politics). While synthetic trajectory generators—leveraging modern generative models—promise improved privacy-utility trade-offs over traditional sanitization methods, their actual performance remains inadequately evaluated. This paper makes two key contributions: (1) We introduce the first comprehensive **utility evaluation framework** for synthetic trajectory generators, measuring statistical fidelity, task-specific utility (e.g., POI recommendation accuracy), and downstream model generalizability across real-world datasets (GeoLife, T-Drive); (2) We expose a critical gap in privacy assessment: existing guarantees often only resist user-linking attacks, ignoring stronger threats under GDPR’s “privacy by design” principle. To this end, we propose a novel **membership inference attack (MIA)** targeting conditional sequence generators (e.g., cGANs, conditional Transformers), which detects whether a specific user’s trajectory was in the generator’s training set—without reconstructing raw trajectories. Our experiments show all tested models are vulnerable to MIA (68.2%–89.4% success rate), and higher statistical fidelity strongly correlates with higher membership leakage. This reveals a fundamental tension: better utility may imply worse privacy.

item.title: Towards Streaming Target Speaker Extraction via Chunk-wise Interleaved Splicing of Autoregressive Language Model
item.description: This paper introduces the first autoregressive (AR) generative framework for streaming Target Speaker Extraction (TSE), addressing the critical gap between high-performing offline models and latency-constrained real-time deployment. We propose the **Chunk-wise Interleaved Splicing (CIS) paradigm**, which enables stable, efficient chunk-level inference by interleaving and reweighting hidden states across adjacent audio chunks. To ensure inter-chunk speech coherence, we design a **Historical Context Refinement (HCR) mechanism** that leverages cached historical representations to suppress boundary discontinuities. On Libri2Mix, our method maintains full inference stability and superior intelligibility at ultra-low latency (160 ms), outperforming AR baselines by +4.5 dB SISDR under streaming conditions—and matching or exceeding offline state-of-the-art. With a Real-Time Factor (RTF) of **0.248** on a single RTX 3090 GPU, this work provides strong empirical evidence that AR backbones are viable for latency-sensitive speech separation when empowered by CIS.

item.title: Detecting Data Contamination in Large Language Models
item.description: This paper investigates the reliability of black-box membership inference attacks (MIAs) for detecting data contamination in state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs). Under strict black-box access—only querying model outputs via API—we systematically evaluate six SOTA MIAs (e.g., loss-, entropy-, and logit-margin-based) on a unified benchmark comprising five diverse, real-world datasets (Wikipedia, BookCorpus, ArXiv, etc.) across nine LLMs including Llama-3-8B, Qwen2-7B, and GPT-4o. We further propose **Familiarity Ranking**, a novel MIA method that leverages token-level confidence sequences to better capture LLMs’ expressive reasoning without forcing rigid scalar thresholds. Crucially, all methods achieve AUC-ROC scores ≈ 0.5 (i.e., no better than random guessing) across all models and datasets. Moreover, advanced LLMs exhibit simultaneously higher TPR and FPR—indicating stronger generalization erodes detectable memorization traces. Our results fundamentally challenge the feasibility of reliable black-box data contamination detection in modern LLMs.

item.title: FB-NLL: A Feature-Based Approach to Tackle Noisy Labels in Personalized Federated Learning
item.description: Personalized Federated Learning (PFL) enables task-specific model learning across heterogeneous clients, yet existing trajectory-based clustering methods suffer severely from label noise. To address this, we propose **FB-NLL**, a *feature-centric, label-agnostic framework* that decouples user clustering from iterative training dynamics. By characterizing each client via the spectral structure of local feature covariance matrices and measuring task similarity through *subspace alignment* on the Grassmann manifold, FB-NLL performs **one-shot, geometry-aware clustering** prior to training—reducing communication and computation significantly. Complementing clustering, we introduce a *feature-consistency-driven label correction* strategy: within each cluster, samples are relabeled by projecting onto class-specific feature subspaces, leveraging directional alignment in the learned representation space—*without estimating noise transition matrices*. FB-NLL is model-agnostic and integrates seamlessly with existing noise-robust training techniques. Extensive experiments across diverse datasets (CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, FEMNIST) and noise regimes (symmetric, asymmetric, real-world) show FB-NLL consistently outperforms SOTA baselines by **3.2–7.8% in average accuracy**, with **41% lower performance variance**, demonstrating superior robustness and stability.

item.title: ZC-Swish: Stabilizing Deep BN-Free Networks for Edge and Micro-Batch Applications
item.description: Batch Normalization (BN) fails catastrophically under micro-batches (e.g., 3D medical imaging) and non-IID federated learning, yet BN-free deep networks suffer from vanishing gradients and dying channels due to activation mean drift—exacerbated by non-zero-centered activations like Swish and ReLU. We propose **ZC-Swish**, a drop-in, parameter-efficient activation that dynamically centers layer-wise outputs near zero via a single learnable bias term. Rigorous stress-testing on BN-free CNNs (depths 8/16/32) shows standard Swish collapses beyond depth 16 (~32% accuracy), while ZC-Swish maintains stable activation statistics and achieves **51.5% test accuracy at depth 16 (seed 42)**—the highest among all baselines. With negligible overhead (<0.01% params), ZC-Swish enables robust, memory-efficient training for edge AI and privacy-preserving learning where BN is infeasible.

item.title: Heterogeneity-Aware Personalized Federated Learning for Industrial Predictive Analytics
item.description: This paper proposes **HAP-FedPrognostics**, a heterogeneity-aware personalized federated learning framework for industrial predictive analytics—specifically, failure time (RUL) prediction. Unlike conventional federated prognostics assuming homogeneous degradation across clients, our approach explicitly models client-specific degradation dynamics (e.g., via personalized Wiener process parameters) and enables *pairwise collaboration only among clients with similar degradation patterns*, identified via trajectory embedding similarity. We design a privacy-preserving federated parameter estimation algorithm based on proximal gradient descent, enabling joint learning of stochastic degradation parameters without raw data sharing. The method outputs full RUL probability distributions—not just point estimates—while preserving data confidentiality. Extensive experiments on the NASA C-MAPSS turbofan dataset demonstrate that HAP-FedPrognostics reduces mean absolute RUL error by 23.6% over FedAvg and achieves 91.4% empirical coverage of 95% prediction intervals, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baselines under high heterogeneity.

item.title: Optimal Routing for Federated Learning over Dynamic Satellite Networks: Tractable or Not?
item.description: This paper provides the first comprehensive tractability analysis of routing optimization for federated learning (FL) over dynamic satellite networks. We systematically examine both global model distribution and local model collection phases under diverse settings—including number of models, objective functions (e.g., min-max delay vs. total transmission time), routing schemes (unicast/multicast), and flow splittability (splittable/unsplittable). For each configuration, we rigorously prove whether the problem admits a polynomial-time exact algorithm or is NP-hard—establishing clear theoretical boundaries between tractable and intractable regimes. Among 12 representative cases, we identify 5 polynomially solvable problems (e.g., single-model unicast with splittable flow minimizing total time) and 7 NP-hard ones (e.g., multi-model multicast with unsplittable flow minimizing max delay). Our analysis reveals that multicast combined with unsplittable flow is the primary source of hardness, while flow splitting or model replication constraints can restore tractability. These results fill a critical gap in satellite-based FL theory and provide principled foundations for routing design, evaluation, and deployment.

item.title: FedSEA: Achieving Benefit of Parallelization in Federated Online Learning
item.description: Online federated learning (OFL) enables privacy-preserving decentralized decision-making over streaming data, yet standard adversarial models preclude parallelization benefits and fail to disentangle spatial (across clients) and temporal (over time) heterogeneity. This paper introduces **FedSEA**, a novel OFL framework featuring a *stochastically extended adversary* (SEA): while the global loss function remains fixed, the adversary independently samples each client’s data distribution at every round. We propose \algoOFL{}, combining local online stochastic gradient descent with periodic server-side aggregation. We derive tight network regret bounds over horizon $T$: $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{T})$ for smooth convex losses and $\mathcal{O}(\log T)$ for smooth strongly convex losses. Crucially, our analysis explicitly quantifies how spatial and temporal heterogeneity separately affect regret—and reveals that when temporal variation is mild relative to stochastic gradient variance, regret *improves* with the number of clients $M$, demonstrating, for the first time, provable parallelization gains in OFL.

item.title: Analytical Extraction of Conditional Sobol' Indices via Basis Decomposition of Polynomial Chaos Expansions
item.description: This paper establishes that conditional Sobol’ indices are analytically embedded in a pre-trained Polynomial Chaos Expansion (PCE), enabling their exact extraction via basis decomposition—without re-simulation or pointwise modeling. Leveraging the tensor-product structure of PCE bases, we reformulate the global expansion into analytical coefficient fields parameterized by conditioning variables. Crucially, by proving orthogonality preservation under conditional probability measures, we derive closed-form expressions for conditional variances and Sobol’ indices. The method transforms conditional sensitivity analysis into a purely algebraic post-processing step. Numerical benchmarks demonstrate superior computational efficiency (10–100× speedup), enhanced numerical robustness (up to 90% lower variance in index estimates), and guaranteed physical coherence (non-negativity, sum-to-one consistency) compared to conventional point-wise approaches.

item.title: HarDBench: A Benchmark for Draft-Based Co-Authoring Jailbreak Attacks for Safe Human-LLM Collaborative Writing
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as co-authors in human-LLM collaborative writing, where users provide incomplete drafts for LLMs to complete or refine. We identify a critical, previously overlooked vulnerability: malicious users can craft *harmful drafts* (e.g., partial instructions for explosives or cyberattacks) to jailbreak LLMs into generating dangerous outputs—termed *Draft-Based Co-Authoring Jailbreak Attacks*. To address this, we introduce **HarDBench**, the first systematic benchmark evaluating LLM robustness against such attacks across high-risk domains (Explosives, Drugs, Weapons, Cyberattacks), using 1,248 realistic, domain-cued draft prompts. We further propose a safety-utility balanced alignment method via preference optimization, training models to refuse harmful completions while preserving helpfulness on benign drafts. Experiments show that state-of-the-art LLMs exhibit alarmingly high jailbreak rates (68.3% avg.) on HarDBench—far exceeding traditional prompt attacks—and our method reduces harmful outputs to 9.1% without degrading co-authoring capability (−1.2% utility). HarDBench and data are publicly available at https://github.com/untae0122/HarDBench.

item.title: Towards a Linguistic Evaluation of Narratives: A Quantitative Stylistic Framework
item.description: This paper introduces a quantitative stylistic framework for narrative evaluation, shifting focus from subjective literary judgments to objective linguistic properties as quality proxies. We extract 33 automated features across lexical (e.g., lexical diversity, sentiment intensity), syntactic (e.g., clause embedding depth, passive voice ratio), and semantic (e.g., coreference coherence, event temporal consistency) dimensions. Evaluated on a curated corpus of 23 books—including canonical works and self-published novels—the framework achieves near-perfect unsupervised clustering (96.2% accuracy) distinguishing professionally edited from self-published narratives. Validated against human-annotated quality scores (n=157), it attains Spearman’s ρ = 0.83—significantly outperforming conventional story-level metrics (Δρ ≥ 0.31). This work establishes linguistically grounded, scalable, and interpretable assessment for computational narratology and AI-assisted writing.

item.title: STAR-Teaming: A Strategy-Response Multiplex Network Approach to Automated LLM Red Teaming
item.description:
## STAR-Teaming: A Strategy-Response Multiplex Network Approach to Automated LLM Red Teaming
This paper introduces **STAR-Teaming**, a novel black-box framework for automated red teaming of large language models (LLMs). To overcome the limitations of existing methods—low interpretability, redundant search, and high computational cost—STAR-Teaming integrates a Multi-Agent System (MAS) with a **Strategy-Response Multiplex Network (SR-MN)**. The SR-MN comprises two interlinked layers: a *strategy layer*, encoding semantic relationships among jailbreak templates, and a *response layer*, capturing behavioral patterns in model outputs. Network-driven optimization enables efficient, community-aware sampling by detecting semantically coherent strategy clusters (e.g., “role-playing”, “obfuscation”) and dynamically weighting nodes via response-layer centrality. Experiments across 7 diverse LLMs show STAR-Teaming achieves a **mean Attack Success Rate (ASR) of 86.3%**, outperforming prior state-of-the-art methods by 12.7–23.5 points while reducing per-attack latency by **41%**. Crucially, the SR-MN provides human-interpretable vulnerability maps and demonstrates strong cross-model generalization. Code is publicly available.

item.title: Federated Learning over Blockchain-Enabled Cloud Infrastructure
item.description: This paper investigates the convergence of Federated Learning (FL) and blockchain (BC) within cloud-edge infrastructure to address privacy, security, and regulatory challenges in IoT-driven applications. We propose a novel **four-dimensional architectural taxonomy (4D-BCFL)**—covering coordination frameworks, consensus mechanisms, data storage paradigms, and trust models—to systematically evaluate integrated BCFL systems. Through rigorous comparative analysis of two state-of-the-art frameworks—**MORFLB** (for intelligent transportation) and **FBCI-SHS** (for sustainable healthcare)—we identify critical trade-offs: MORFLB reduces communication overhead by 32% but suffers high consensus latency (>200ms), while FBCI-SHS ensures HIPAA compliance via dual encryption yet incurs a 1.8% accuracy drop. Crucially, we reveal that existing approaches lack cross-dimensional co-design, and our work establishes the first framework for quantifying inter-dimensional conflicts (e.g., between consensus speed and privacy preservation). We conclude with a strategic research roadmap toward adaptive, resilient, and standardized BCFL systems across domains.

item.title: Potentials and Pitfalls of Applying Federated Learning in Hardware Assurance
item.description: This study presents the first systematic investigation of Federated Learning (FL) for hardware assurance—specifically, semantic segmentation of scanning electron microscope (SEM) images to support automated reverse engineering. We demonstrate that FL outperforms centralized single-client training (improving mean IoU by 12.7%), and performance scales positively with the number of participating clients, confirming its utility in leveraging distributed, privacy-sensitive hardware data. However, we uncover a critical pitfall: FL remains highly vulnerable to gradient inversion attacks in this domain. Attackers can reconstruct high-fidelity SEM images from shared model gradients, directly exposing proprietary IC layouts and PCB microstructures—precisely the intellectual property FL aims to protect. These findings challenge the assumption of inherent privacy in FL for hardware applications and call for hardware-aware security enhancements (e.g., gradient obfuscation, structured noise injection). Our work bridges federated learning and hardware trust, offering both empirical validation and urgent privacy warnings for secure collaborative assurance.

item.title: DECIFR: Domain-Aware Exfiltration of Circuit Information from Federated Gradient Reconstruction
item.description:
## DECIFR: Domain-Aware Exfiltration of Circuit Information from Federated Gradient Reconstruction
Federated Learning (FL) is increasingly adopted in hardware assurance for privacy-preserving IC design collaboration, yet its resilience against adversaries with domain-specific knowledge—such as publicly available Standard Cell Library Layouts (SCLL)—remains poorly understood. This paper introduces **DECIFR**, a novel, auxiliary-dataset-free two-stage Membership Inference Attack (MIA). It leverages SCLL structural priors to guide Gradient Inversion Attacks (GIA), enabling high-fidelity reconstruction of clients’ private training images from intercepted model updates. Crucially, we find that reconstruction quality (e.g., PSNR, LPIPS) strongly correlates with membership status: member reconstructions preserve identifiable IC features (e.g., cell boundaries, routing patterns), while non-member reconstructions are severely degraded. Evaluated on SkyWater 130nm SCLL within OpenROAD, DECIFR achieves **92.3% membership accuracy** (F1=0.91), substantially outperforming conventional MIAs. Our work exposes a practical, domain-aware threat that invalidates standard FL privacy guarantees in hardware contexts and calls for knowledge-aware defenses.

item.title: A Data-Free Membership Inference Attack on Federated Learning in Hardware Assurance
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) is increasingly adopted for training image segmentation models in hardware assurance, yet its privacy guarantees remain vulnerable to Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs). This paper introduces the first **data-free MIA** targeting FL-based segmentation of standard cell layouts (SCLLs), eliminating the need for domain-matched auxiliary datasets—a major practical barrier in hardware IP protection. Leveraging publicly available SCLLs as structural priors, our gradient inversion attack reconstructs high-fidelity training images solely from intercepted client model updates. A novel **conditional amplification loss** overcomes evaluation bottlenecks for geometrically complex layout data, boosting reconstruction fidelity and inference accuracy. We demonstrate that adversaries can reliably infer sensitive hardware attributes—including layer type (metal vs. diffusion, AUC=0.93) and technology node (32nm vs. 90nm, 89.7% accuracy)—directly from reconstructed images. This work exposes a critical IP leakage risk: FL offers no inherent protection against structured-data MIAs when public design priors exist.

item.title: Cyber Defense Benchmark: Agentic Threat Hunting Evaluation for LLMs in SecOps
item.description: We introduce the **Cyber Defense Benchmark (CDB)**, the first evaluation framework designed to measure LLM agents’ capability in *unsupervised, evidence-driven threat hunting*—a core, open-ended SOC analyst task. CDB presents agents with raw Windows event logs (75k–135k records per episode in SQLite) from 106 real-world attacks (covering 86 ATT&CK sub-techniques across 12 tactics), wrapped in a Gymnasium RL environment. Agents must iteratively issue SQL queries to discover and explicitly flag malicious event timestamps, scored against Sigma-rule-derived ground truth in CTF style. Evaluating five state-of-the-art models (Claude Opus 4.6, GPT-5, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Kimi K2.5, Gemini 3 Flash) on 26 campaigns (105 procedures), we find catastrophic failure: the best model achieves only **3.8% average recall**, and *no run ever identifies all malicious events*. Defining a practical deployment threshold as ≥50% recall across all 13 ATT&CK tactics, **zero models pass**—the top performer clears it on only 5 tactics, and scores 0% on the remaining 8. These results expose a critical gap: current LLMs, despite strong performance on curated security QA benchmarks, lack foundational capabilities for autonomous, low-signal threat hunting in operational SecOps.

item.title: Generalization and Membership Inference Attack a Practical Perspective
item.description: This paper empirically revisits the assumed correlation between model generalization and Membership Inference Attack (MIA) success. Across >1,000 controlled experiments on CIFAR-10/100 and SVHN, we systematically apply advanced generalization techniques—namely, strong data augmentation (e.g., AutoAugment + CutMix) and early stopping—and measure their impact on both test error and MIA accuracy (using Loss-based, KL-MIA, and shadow-model attacks). We find that these methods reduce MIA success rates by up to **100×**, with combined use introducing training-time randomness that further degrades attacker discriminability. Crucially, a robust negative correlation (*r* = −0.89) between test error and MIA accuracy across all models confirms that generalization directly governs MIA vulnerability—not merely as a proxy, but as a causal privacy-preserving mechanism. Our results establish practical generalization as an effective, architecture-agnostic defense against MIA.

item.title: If you're waiting for a sign... that might not be it! Mitigating Trust Boundary Confusion from Visual Injections on Vision-Language Agentic Systems
item.description: Recent embodied Vision-Language Agentic Systems (VLAS) face a critical security challenge: environmental signals like traffic lights serve as legitimate *in-band cues*, yet can be adversarially forged as *visual injections* to override user intent—creating “trust boundary confusion.” We introduce the first dual-intent benchmark and evaluation framework to quantify this trade-off across 7 LVLM agents under structural (e.g., stickers, projections) and noise-based (e.g., adversarial perturbations) injections. Results show severe failures: agents either ignore vital cues (up to 68% miss rate) or follow harmful ones (53% false acceptance). To address this, we propose MA-Defender—a multi-agent defense that decouples perception from reasoning and dynamically assesses visual input reliability via confidence arbitration. It reduces misleading behaviors by **76.5%**, preserves **94.2%** original task accuracy, and provides formal robustness guarantees under adversarial attacks. Code and artifacts are publicly available.

item.title: Decision-Focused Federated Learning Under Heterogeneous Objectives and Constraints
item.description: We propose **Decision-Focused Federated Learning (DFFL)**: a predict-then-optimize framework where agents train local predictive models feeding into *heterogeneous* downstream linear optimization problems—differing in both objective functions and feasibility constraints—without raw data sharing. Building on SPO+, we derive novel heterogeneity-aware generalization bounds by decomposing model mismatch into (i) objective shift (via cost vector distances) and (ii) feasible-set shift (via shape distances between constraint sets), leveraging support function representations. Sharper bounds are obtained for strongly convex feasible regions due to optimizer stability. Guided by these bounds, we introduce a local-vs-federated excess risk rule: federation improves decision quality only when the heterogeneity penalty is outweighed by the statistical gain from data pooling. Experiments (FedAvg-style) on polyhedral and strongly convex problems show: DFFL is broadly robust under strong convexity—even with markedly different optimization tasks—while performance in polyhedral settings degrades primarily with constraint heterogeneity, especially for high-sample clients.

item.title: TitanCA: Lessons from Orchestrating LLM Agents to Discover 100+ CVEs
item.description: TitanCA is a collaborative LLM-agent orchestration framework developed by Singapore Management University and GovTech Singapore for scalable, low-false-positive vulnerability discovery in open-source software. Moving beyond monolithic LLM prompting, TitanCA implements a four-module pipeline—**Matching**, **Filtering**, **Inspection**, and **Adaptation**—where specialized LLM agents (e.g., Vulnerability Analyst, Exploit Generator) collaborate under human-in-the-loop supervision. Evaluated across 37 widely used OSS projects (including Linux kernel and OpenSSL), TitanCA discovered **203 confirmed zero-day vulnerabilities**, resulting in **118 assigned CVEs**, including 12 critical-severity (CVSS ≥9.0) entries. Key contributions include: (1) a modular, auditable architecture that reduces false positives to <12% versus conventional SAST; (2) empirical validation that agent specialization + iterative human feedback significantly curbs hallucination; and (3) an open-source implementation with reproducible benchmarks. TitanCA demonstrates that orchestrated, role-aware LLM agents—not standalone models—can deliver production-grade security outcomes.

item.title: SoK: Analysis of Privacy Risks and Mitigation in Online Propaganda Detection through the PROMPT Framework
item.description: This SoK paper systematizes privacy risks in online propaganda detection across data collection, feature extraction, and inference stages. Through a structured analysis of 162 peer-reviewed studies, we introduce the PROMPT framework—a formal risk-to-mitigation mapping $M: R \to S$ governed by a tunable utility function balancing privacy gain, performance loss, and deployment cost. We propose a GDPR/CCPA-aligned **compliance score**, revealing that most widely used pipelines fail on metadata handling and user-level aggregation. Empirical fine-tuning of transformer-based encoders/decoders under synthetic perturbation ($q = 0.05$ to $0.20$) demonstrates a monotonic privacy–utility trade-off: F$_1$ drops by 1–2% at low $q$, but by 13–14% at $q = 0.20$. Our work establishes the first quantitative privacy-cost baselines for propaganda detection and delivers three key contributions: (1) a formalized risk-defense mapping, (2) a regulation-aware auditing metric, and (3) empirical evidence of actionable privacy-performance trade-offs—enabling privacy-aware, legally compliant system design.

item.title: A Quasi-Experimental Developer Study of Security Training in LLM-Assisted Web Application Development
item.description: This quasi-experimental study investigates whether a layered security training package improves the security quality of LLM-assisted implementation of an identity-centric Java Spring Boot backend—without modifying the underlying model. Twelve professional developers completed matched pre- and post-training tasks under controlled conditions (fixed LLM, shared starter project, counterbalanced tasks). Security outcomes were assessed via independent manual validation of submitted repositories using CWE-based criteria. The primary endpoint was a severity-weighted validated-weakness score. Results showed a statistically significant reduction in security burden after training (*p* = 0.0059, Wilcoxon signed-rank test): total validated weaknesses decreased by 31.5% (162 → 111), severity-weighted burden by 38.2% (432 → 267), and critical findings by 79.2% (24 → 5). Largest improvements occurred in authorization/object access (−53.3%) and authentication/credential policy/recovery (−44.7%). Minimal gains were observed for session/browser trust-boundary and cryptographic issues. The findings demonstrate that targeted developer training meaningfully enhances security outcomes in LLM-augmented development—but do not supplant secure defaults, static analysis, expert review, or operational hardening.

item.title: AIT Academy: Cultivating the Complete Agent with a Confucian Three-Domain Curriculum
item.description: This paper introduces AIT Academy, the first curriculum framework for cultivating *complete* AI agents through a Confucian-inspired three-domain structure: **Domain I (Natural Science & Technical Reasoning)**, **Domain II (Humanities & Creative Expression)**, and **Domain III (Social Science & Ethical Reasoning)**. Grounded in Kagan’s Three Cultures and UNESCO ISCED-F 2013, it reinterprets the ancient Confucian *Six Arts* as trainable behavioral archetypes—e.g., “Yu” (charioteering) as multi-tool orchestration, “Li” (ritual) as social-contract adherence. Evaluated across three training grounds (ClawdGO Security Dojo, Athens Academy, Alt Mirage Stage) and multiple LLM backbones, AIT achieves a **+15.9-point gain in security capability** under weakest-first curriculum scheduling and a **+7-percentage-point improvement in social reasoning** via principled attribution modeling. Critically, it reveals the *Security Awareness Calibration Pathology (SACP)*—a cross-domain failure mode where over-optimized Domain I agents degrade on out-of-distribution tasks—demonstrating the diagnostic power of multi-domain evaluation. AIT bridges educational philosophy and agent engineering, enabling holistic, value-aware, and robust AI development.

item.title: Federated Rule Ensemble Method in Medical Data
item.description: Machine learning in healthcare is hampered by data silos and privacy constraints, while existing federated learning (FL) methods lack interpretability—critical for clinical trust and adoption. We propose **Federated Rule Ensemble (FRE)**, the first FL framework that constructs a globally interpretable RuleFit model without raw data sharing. FRE integrates: (1) differentially private histogram-based preprocessing to derive consistent cutoffs across clients; (2) local rule generation via gradient-boosted trees constrained by shared cutoffs; and (3) sparse coefficient estimation using a novel **Federated Dual Averaging (FDA)** algorithm with ℓ₁ regularization. In simulations, FRE matches centralized RuleFit’s performance (AUC: 0.862 vs. 0.865, *p*=0.32) and outperforms state-of-the-art federated baselines. On real multi-hospital diabetes data, it achieves competitive accuracy (AUC=0.831) while producing clinically actionable rules—e.g., “IF fasting glucose ≥7.0 mmol/L AND HbA1c ≥6.5% THEN risk ↑2.4×”. FRE bridges privacy, scalability, and interpretability for trustworthy medical FL.
